## [03:07 - 06:10]

And the way it works, so list is basically the list and back then it would be like direct mail usually or you might be advertising in a magazine or a newspaper but usually a lot of times direct mail. So it'd be the list that you were mailing. A lot of times you would rent a list or buy a list and send that direct mail. So you would always have to worry about getting the right list because if you had a list of people that were interested in fly fishing and you tried to teach or sell them something about playing guitar, you were probably not gonna get great results because some of those fly fisher people were probably interested in guitar but not many of them, not all of them. So you had to get the right list and then offer is like what are you actually offering them? What are they, what are you gonna give them? What do they get if you, if when they place an order with you, when they buy your stuff? And then copy is the sales copy and back then it was usually direct marketing like letters, sales letters, but it would be like printed out and sent to them or in an advertisement. So there was list, offering, copy and the way it worked is if you could actually not get one of these right, you could sort of screw up on one of these and do okay. So if you had the right list and the right offer but you didn't have great copy, you could still be okay. And if, or if you had a lousy offer but you had the right list and you had great copy, you'd probably be okay. You might get a lot of returns but you probably make a lot of sales. And then if you didn't really have a list, if you, or if you just had a list, a general list and it wasn't very targeted, but you had an incredible offer and an incredible copy, then you'd be okay. So I like to think, to give an example, Gary Halber, who, you know, a legendary copywriter who passed away several years ago, legendary direct marketing copywriter. And I actually got to meet Gary on a few occasions and go out to dinner with him once. So that was a, that was really cool, brilliant genius. And he had the example of like, if you had a hamburger stand, what is the one thing that you would want more than anything else in your marketing? And he's, he said, well, you have a hamburger stand. You could take all the copy in the world and throw it away. If you had a crowd of starving people, it doesn't matter. It doesn't really matter how good your food was. It doesn't matter how you advertise it, what the copy was. So long as you had a starving, like just, they, they're voracious, you're going to sell a lot of hamburgers, right? So you have the right offer at the right time. On the other hand, like if you have some people that are just mildly hungry, but you have just a fantastic offer, you got free hamburgers and you advertise in a really great way, you're probably going to be able to, you know, either give away or sell a bunch of food. Okay. And then the final one was like, if you had hungry people and you had a bunch of burgers, you probably don't need good copies.
